nuance: たら is the most versatile conditional — the second clause follows once the first is realized; works with a single, one-off event.

examples.en: When I arrive at the station, I'll call you.
If I have time, I want to go to Hokkaido.

nuance: ば states a general or logical if-then; the main clause is often a wish or a natural result.

examples.en: If it's cheap, I'll rent this room.
If you practice every day, your Japanese will get better.

nuance: なら picks up something the other person just raised — 'if that's the topic, then...'.

examples.en: If it's hot springs (you're after), Hakone is famous.
If you're looking for a cheap room, a share house is good.

nuance: と marks an automatic, always-true result — turn this and that happens. No command or request may follow.

examples.en: When you press this button, a ticket comes out.
When spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom.
Even if it rains, I'll go to work.
Even if it's expensive, I'll buy these shoes.

meaning: come to / reach the point where
connection: V-dict or V-ない + ようになる
nuance: Marks a gradual change ending in a new ability or habit that wasn't there before.

examples.en: I've reached the point where I can read a Japanese newspaper.
I've started running every morning (as a habit).

meaning: make an effort to / see to it that
connection: V-dict or V-ない + ようにする

examples.en: I make an effort to eat vegetables every day.
Please see to it that you're not late for the meeting.

nuance: Expresses an intention just decided on; とおもっている describes an ongoing plan.

examples.en: I think I'll go to Akihabara this weekend.
I'm thinking of buying a car next year.

nuance: The casual counterpart of 〜ましょう — used with friends to suggest doing something.

examples.en: Let's drink at the izakaya tonight.
I'm tired, so let's head home soon.
I'm planning to move house next month.
The meeting is scheduled from three o'clock.

connection: plain form + かもしれません

examples.en: It might snow tomorrow.
The store manager might have already gone home.

nuance: はず expresses a logical expectation based on evidence — not obligation.

examples.en: He should have arrived at the airport by now.
This train should stop at Shinjuku.

nuance: Reports second-hand information; attaches to the plain form, unlike the look-like そう.

examples.en: According to the forecast, I hear it'll rain tomorrow.
I heard that ramen shop is delicious.

nuance: Describes an impression from what you see; drops the final い of i-adjectives (おいしい→おいしそう).

examples.en: This cake looks delicious.
It looks like it's about to rain any moment.

nuance: A reasoned inference from evidence; more formal than みたい.

examples.en: The road is wet. It seems it rained during the night.
It seems the person next door isn't home.

nuance: The casual spoken equivalent of ようだ; also expresses resemblance ('like a…').

examples.en: It seems like he caught a cold.
This room is cold like a fridge.

nuance: Reports hearsay you judge reliable; after a noun it means 'befitting / typical of'.

examples.en: Apparently a new shop has opened in front of the station.
Today is a warm, spring-like day.

nuance: The 'suffering passive' often implies the action was unwelcome to the subject.

examples.en: I got my foot stepped on on the train.
I was asked my name by the store manager.

nuance: Ru-verbs take 〜られる; casually the ら drops (食べれる). The object usually takes が.

examples.en: I can eat sashimi.
With this app you can buy tickets.

nuance: Means both 'make (force)' and 'let (allow)' — context and particles (に/を) disambiguate.

examples.en: The manager made me work overtime.
I let the child play in the park.

nuance: Combines causative + passive: someone forced you, and you found it unpleasant.

examples.en: I was made to wait two hours by the department head.
I was forced to do homework every day.

nuance: A gentle command from a superior to an inferior — parent to child, teacher to student.

examples.en: Go to sleep now.
Eat your vegetables too.

nuance: A rough, forceful 'don't' — mostly male speech, warnings, or signs.

examples.en: Don't take photos here.
It's dangerous, so don't run on the platform.

nuance: Marks completion or, very often, regret over something that happened. Casual: 〜ちゃう.

examples.en: I went and lost my wallet.
I ended up eating the whole cake.

meaning: do in advance / leave as is
nuance: Do something ahead of time in preparation. Casual contraction: 〜とく.

examples.en: I'll book the hotel in advance before the trip.
Please leave the window open.

nuance: Do something to see how it goes — a trial, not necessarily a struggle.

examples.en: I'll try eating this shop's ramen.
I tried calling the ward office.

nuance: You do something for someone else's benefit; can sound condescending, so use with care.

examples.en: I showed my friend the way.
I carried her luggage for her.

nuance: Someone does something for me or my in-group; carries a sense of gratitude.

examples.en: The shop clerk showed me the way.
My friend came all the way to the airport for me.

meaning: have someone do / receive the favor
nuance: I receive the favor of an action; the doer is marked with に.

examples.en: I had my friend help me move.
I had my teacher correct my essay.

meaning: go on doing / change away from now
nuance: A change moving away from the present into the future, or an action done then leaving.

examples.en: I'll keep studying Japanese from now on.
In the morning I'll buy tea at the konbini and head off.

meaning: come to / change up to now
nuance: A change that has developed up to the present, or going to do something and coming back.

examples.en: It has gradually gotten cold.
I'll go buy a bento at the konbini (and come back).

nuance: A transitive verb's result left in place on purpose; the object usually takes が.

examples.en: A map has been put up on the wall.
Beer has been chilled in the fridge.
I started learning Japanese last month.
The cherry blossoms have started to bloom.
I finished reading the book.
Once I finish the homework, I'll sleep.

nuance: Conjugates like an i-adjective (〜やすかった). The full kanji 易い is rarely written.

examples.en: This pen is easy to write with.
Shinjuku station is easy to get lost in.

nuance: The opposite of 〜やすい; conjugates like an i-adjective.

examples.en: This kanji is hard to read.
The streets in front of the station are hard to figure out.

meaning: sometimes happens / there are times when
nuance: With a dictionary verb it means an occasional occurrence; do not confuse it with V-た + ことがある (past experience).

examples.en: There are times when I miss the last train.
Because of overtime, it sometimes gets late at night.

connection: V-dict / V-ない + ことにする
nuance: The speaker's own decision. Contrast with ことになる, where the decision comes from outside.

examples.en: I've decided to study Japanese every morning.
I've decided not to eat out this month.

nuance: The outcome is decided by circumstances or others, not the speaker's own choice.

examples.en: It's been decided that I'll work in Hokkaido from next month.
It's been decided that the meeting will be next week.
I want to go to a ramen shop called "Ichiran."
I heard the word "share house" for the first time.

nuance: Lists a couple of representative examples casually, implying there are others.

examples.en: At the convenience store I buy things like rice balls and tea.
On weekends I spend the time doing things like taking a walk.
I showed them around Asakusa, Shibuya, and so on.
There are things like books on the desk.

nuance: Lists reasons or qualities, often implying "and on top of that."

examples.en: This room is cheap, and what's more it's close to the station.
It's raining today, so (and what's more) I'll stay home.

nuance: Carries a sense of surprise, dissatisfaction, or unexpected contrast.

examples.en: Even though I made a reservation, the restaurant was closed.
Even though it's Sunday, the train is crowded.

nuance: Presents the reason as objective fact; softer and more polite than から.

examples.en: Because my head hurts, I'll go home early.
Because it's my first time, I don't know where the ward office is.

nuance: A more formal, softer version of けど; connects two clauses with contrast.

examples.en: Japanese is difficult, however it's interesting.
I went to the izakaya in front of the station, but it was full.

meaning: there is a (smell / sound / taste / feeling)
nuance: Used with perception nouns like におい, 音, 味, 気; describes something sensed, not deliberately done.

examples.en: There's a nice smell coming from the kitchen.
There's a strange sound coming from the room next door.

connection: V-ている / N-の + 間に
nuance: 間に marks a point within a span when something happens; 間 alone marks the whole duration.

examples.en: While I was sleeping, there was an earthquake.
During the summer break, I went to Hokkaido.

nuance: Marks a continuous action up to an end point. Contrast with までに (a deadline).

examples.en: I work my part-time job until ten at night.
I'll go to Shinjuku by train.

nuance: A deadline by which an action must be completed. Contrast with まで (continuous up to a point).

examples.en: I'll submit the documents by Friday.
I want to get to the station by the last train.

connection: N + ばかり / V-て + ばかり
nuance: Emphasizes that it's always the same thing, often with a critical tone.

examples.en: Every day I eat nothing but ramen.
My little brother does nothing but play games.

nuance: Only a short time has passed since the action, in the speaker's feeling. Contrast with たところ (objective timing).

examples.en: I just came to Japan, so I don't have any friends yet.
I just bought this smartphone.

meaning: about to / in the middle of / just did
connection: V-dict / V-ている / V-た + ところ
nuance: The verb form sets the timing: dict = about to, ている = in the middle of, た = just finished.

examples.en: I'm just about to leave the house now.
I've just finished eating lunch.

nuance: A state remains unchanged while another action happens, often unintentionally.

examples.en: I fell asleep with the lights left on.
I went out leaving the window open.

nuance: The te-form of the negative; expresses a negative reason or cause for what follows.

examples.en: I couldn't read the kanji and so I was in trouble.
The train didn't come and so I was late.
I don't know whether or not I passed the interview.
I'll check whether or not this shop is open.

nuance: A question word plus か embeds a whole question inside a larger sentence.

examples.en: Please tell me where the station is.
I asked how much the rent is.

connection: plain form + と言っていました
nuance: Reports what someone else said. と言っていました reports ongoing/general speech; と言いました a single utterance.

examples.en: The store manager said that tomorrow is a day off.
My friend said that Hokkaido is cold.

nuance: Expresses purpose with a volitional verb; with a noun it means "for the sake of."

examples.en: I save money for a trip.
In order to get a visa, I gather the documents.

nuance: Purpose with a non-volitional verb (potential or negative); contrast with ために, used with volitional verbs.

examples.en: I practice every day so that I can read kanji.
I leave early so that I don't miss the last train.

meaning: I wish I had / should have
nuance: Expresses regret over something not done. Add なければ for regret over something that was done.

examples.en: I wish I had brought an umbrella.
I should have made a reservation sooner.

nuance: A soft suggestion or advice. Can sound pushy if overused with someone of higher status.

examples.en: If you're tired, how about resting a little?
How about trying to ask the station staff?

nuance: A non-contiguous pattern: そんなに sets up the degree, and the negative verb/adjective completes it.

examples.en: It's not that cold today.
This izakaya isn't that expensive.

nuance: A casual self-directed wondering, often spoken to oneself.

examples.en: I wonder if it'll be sunny tomorrow.
I wonder if this is the right train.

nuance: The plain-form conjecture; でしょう is its polite equivalent.

examples.en: It'll probably be crowded on the weekend.
He's probably already gone home.

nuance: Attaches to a quantity to mean "in equal portions" or "a certain amount at a time."

examples.en: I memorize a little kanji at a time every day.
Each person got two rice balls each.

nuance: Regular sonkeigo: raises the subject (a customer, a boss). The verb stem sits between お and になる — e.g. お読みになる (reads).

examples.en: The store manager has already gone home.
Will the president read this book?

nuance: Regular kenjougo: lowers your own action toward someone you respect. お待ちする (I wait), お持ちする (I carry it for you).

examples.en: I'll carry your luggage (for you).
I'll wait for you in the lobby.

reading: とくべつなそんけいご
meaning: special honorific verbs (いらっしゃる, 召し上がる…)
nuance: Irregular sonkeigo replaces whole verbs: いる/行く/来る→いらっしゃる, 食べる/飲む→召し上がる, 言う→おっしゃる, する→なさる, 見る→ご覧になる.

examples.en: What would you like to eat, sir/madam?
The teacher will come here tomorrow.

reading: とくべつなけんじょうご
meaning: special humble verbs (参る, いただく, 申す…)
nuance: Irregular kenjougo lowers your own action: 行く/来る→参る, 食べる/飲む/もらう→いただく, 言う→申す, する→いたす, 見る→拝見する.

examples.en: My name is Tanaka.
I will come to the office at ten o'clock.

nuance: Polite request built on the sonkeigo frame — softer and more respectful than the plain 〜てください. お待ちください = please wait.

examples.en: Please wait a moment.
Please have a seat here.

nuance: The most polite copula — shop and hotel staff use it. でございます replaces です; ございます alone can replace あります.

examples.en: This is the new product.
Reception is on the second floor.
This umbrella is the same as mine.
My room is the same size as his.

nuance: You can't state another person's inner feelings directly; がる reports the outward signs — 寒がる = looks/acts cold. Drops the final い/な of the adjective.

examples.en: My roommate always seems to feel cold.
The child is afraid of the dog.

nuance: The がる of feelings applied to 〜たい: reports that a third person shows they want to do something. You use 〜たい for yourself, 〜たがる for others.

examples.en: My little brother wants to go to Akihabara.
My friend wanted to see the photos of the izakaya.
Sorry for being late.
I'm sorry for calling so many times.

nuance: よかった is the past of いい — expresses relief/gladness that something happened. The negative 〜なくてよかった = glad I didn't.

examples.en: I'm glad I brought an umbrella.
I'm glad I moved into this share house.

nuance: You want someone ELSE to do the action (marked with に). Contrast 〜たい (you want to do it yourself).

examples.en: I want the shop clerk to tell me the way.
I want the landlord to fix the air conditioner.

nuance: 必要 is a na-adjective/noun meaning "necessity". With a dictionary verb it states that doing X is required; the negative 必要はない = don't need to.

examples.en: You need to register your address at the ward office.
You need to write a résumé before the interview.

nuance: Different from 見られる/聞ける (ability): 見える/聞こえる mean something comes into view or reaches the ear on its own, without effort.

examples.en: You can see Tokyo Tower from the room's window.
I can hear music from the room next door.

nuance: Sets up a request or problem gently, then trails off — the listener infers the ask. Common when approaching staff: "道に迷ったんですが…" (I'm lost, and so…).

examples.en: I've gotten lost — which way is the station?
I'd like to make a reservation — are you open tonight?

nuance: Placed after a number/amount, も stresses that it is surprisingly large. Contrast with しか…ない (only, surprisingly little).

examples.en: I went to as many as five interviews.
This coat cost a whole 30,000 yen.

connection: V-ない stem + なくてはいけません
nuance: Drop the ない, add なくてはいけません. Same "must" as なければならない; more conversational. The casual contraction is なくちゃ.

ja.t: 起きなくてはいけません
f: きなくてはいけません

examples.en: I have to get up early tomorrow.

ja.t: 分けなくてはいけません
f: けなくてはいけません

examples.en: You have to separate the trash.

connection: casual contractions of なければ/なくては
nuance: なきゃ ← なければ, なくちゃ ← なくては. The trailing verb (いけない/ならない) is usually dropped: "もう行かなきゃ" = gotta go now.

examples.en: I gotta head home now.
I've gotta pay the rent.

nuance: 場合 = "situation/case"; presents a hypothetical or contingency, often on signs and instructions. 地震の場合は = in the event of an earthquake.

examples.en: In the case of an earthquake, please don't use the elevator.
If you're going to be late, please call the shop.

nuance: Marks a degree or rough amount — "to the extent that" after a verb, or "about/approximately" after a quantity. Level is disputed between N4 and N3 lists, so filed at the lower boundary.

examples.en: It takes about ten minutes to the station.
My legs were so tired I could hardly stand.
